summary: Joel Spolsky introduces his Strategy Letter V by observing that companies investing heavily in open source software are motivated by business strategy, not idealism. He frames open source through the lens of microeconomic complements, suggesting that companies support open source when it commoditizes products that complement their own revenue-generating offerings. This is a teaser post pointing readers to the full essay.
key_points: Companies invest in open source primarily for strategic business reasons, not ideological ones
The microeconomic principle of complements explains corporate open source investment
Open source advocacy by large companies should be understood as rational self-interest, not altruism
money_quotes: Most of the companies spending big money to develop open source software are doing it because it's a good business strategy for them, not because they suddenly stopped believing in capitalism and fell in love with freedom-as-in-speech.
key_insight: Corporate open source investment is best understood through the economics of complements, not through the lens of ideology or altruism.
